questions.to_find: What values does the program print after three swap calls?

understand.plain: The program has three swap mechanisms: swap1 is a macro (textual substitution), swap2 passes integers by value (copies), and swap3 passes pointers (actual references). A macro actually modifies the original variables, pass-by-value does not, and pass-by-pointer does. We trace what num1 and num2 are after each swap.

keywords.explain: A #define macro does textual substitution at compile time; the code is inlined directly, so it operates on the actual variables in the caller's scope.
keywords.example: swap1(num1,num2) expands to: tmp=num1; num1=num2; num2=tmp; — this actually swaps num1 and num2.

keywords.explain: When arguments are passed by value, the function receives copies of the variables. Modifying them inside the function does not affect the originals.
keywords.example: swap2(5, 4) swaps local copies a and b, but num1 and num2 in main remain unchanged.

keywords.explain: Passing the address of a variable allows the function to dereference and modify the original variable.
keywords.example: swap3(&num1, &num2) dereferences the pointers and actually swaps the values of num1 and num2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Trace of num1, num2</text><text x='30' y='65' class='sub ink'>Initial:</text><text x='150' y='65' class='sub ink'>num1=5, num2=4</text><text x='30' y='95' class='sub ink'>After swap1 (macro):</text><text x='230' y='95' class='sub ok'>num1=4, num2=5  [SWAPPED]</text><text x='30' y='125' class='sub ink'>After swap2 (by-value):</text><text x='230' y='125' class='sub no'>num1=4, num2=5  [NO CHANGE]</text><text x='30' y='155' class='sub ink'>After swap3 (by-pointer):</text><text x='230' y='155' class='sub ok'>num1=5, num2=4  [SWAPPED]</text><text x='30' y='182' class='tag ink'>printf prints: 4 5 / 4 5 / 5 4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table tracing num1 and num2 through each swap: after macro swap they become 4,5; after value swap they remain 4,5; after pointer swap they become 5,4.

given.aim: Determine the final printed output of the C program after three different swap operations.

terms.meaning: Starting values of the two integers in main.
terms.connects: These are the variables being swapped.

terms.meaning: Textual substitution — effectively executes the swap code directly in main.
terms.example: tmp=num1; num1=num2; num2=tmp; — actual swap happens.
terms.connects: After swap1: num1=4, num2=5. printf prints '4 5'.

terms.meaning: Copies of num1 and num2 are passed; the originals are not modified.
terms.example: Inside swap2, a and b are local copies. Swapping them doesn't affect num1, num2.
terms.connects: After swap2: num1=4, num2=5 (unchanged). printf prints '4 5'.

terms.meaning: Addresses of num1 and num2 are passed; dereferencing swaps the originals.
terms.connects: After swap3: num1=5, num2=4. printf prints '5 4'.

given.plan: Trace num1 and num2 through each swap call step by step. swap1 (macro) swaps them to 4,5. swap2 (by-value) does nothing to originals, stays 4,5. swap3 (by-pointer) swaps them back to 5,4. The three printf lines output: '4 5', '4 5', '5 4'.

steps.title: Apply swap1 (macro) to num1=5, num2=4
steps.apply: Macro expands inline: tmp=num1(=5); num1=num2(=4); num2=tmp(=5). Result: num1=4, num2=5. printf("%d %d", num1, num2) prints '4 5'.
steps.note: The macro uses the global tmp variable (declared in main), so it correctly swaps.

steps.title: Apply swap2 (pass-by-value) to num1=4, num2=5
steps.formula_raw: void swap2(int a, int b) — copies passed
steps.apply: swap2 receives copies: a=4, b=5. Inside: tmp=4; a=5; b=4. The originals num1 and num2 are untouched. Result: num1=4, num2=5. printf prints '4 5'.
steps.note: Classic pass-by-value: changes to local parameters do not propagate back to caller.

steps.title: Apply swap3 (pass-by-pointer) to num1=4, num2=5
steps.apply: swap3 receives &num1 and &num2. tmp=*a(=4); *a=*b(=5); *b=tmp(=4). Now num1=5, num2=4. printf prints '5 4'.
steps.note: Pass-by-pointer correctly modifies the caller's variables through dereferencing.

steps.title: Identify the matching answer option
steps.apply: The three output lines are: '4 5', '4 5', '5 4'. Looking at the given answer options, option B shows '5,4' as the final state — which corresponds to num1=5, num2=4 after swap3. The sequence described matches option B.
steps.note: Option B: 5,4 refers to the final values of num1 and num2 after all swaps.

solution.result: B. 5,4 — The program prints '4 5', '4 5', '5 4'; final values of num1 and num2 are 5 and 4 respectively.

questions.formula_note: Macro expansion (textual substitution) vs pass-by-value vs pass-by-pointer: only macros and pointer-based swaps affect the caller's variables.

questions.question:
The most appropriate matching for the following pairs is:

X: m = malloc(5); m = NULL;
   1: using dangling pointers
Y: free(a); a -> value = 5;
   2: using uninitialized pointers
Z: char *p; *p = 'a';
   3: lost memory

A. X-1, Y-3, Z-2
B. X-2, Y-1, Z-3
C. X-3, Y-2, Z-1
D. X-3, Y-1, Z-2
questions.to_find: Match pointer misuse scenarios X, Y, Z with the correct pointer error types 1, 2, 3.

understand.plain: Three pointer-related code snippets are matched against three types of pointer errors. X allocates memory and then assigns NULL to the pointer — the allocated memory is now unreachable (lost). Y frees memory and then accesses it — the pointer now points to freed memory (dangling). Z declares a pointer but never initializes it and then dereferences it — using an uninitialized pointer.

keywords.explain: A pointer that points to memory that has already been freed or deallocated. Accessing it leads to undefined behavior.
keywords.example: free(a); a->value = 5; — 'a' is a dangling pointer after free().

keywords.explain: When allocated heap memory is no longer accessible because the only pointer to it has been overwritten or lost.
keywords.example: m = malloc(5); m = NULL; — the 5 bytes allocated are now unreachable.

keywords.explain: A pointer variable that has been declared but not assigned any valid address before being dereferenced.
keywords.example: char *p; *p = 'a'; — p has a garbage address; writing to *p is undefined.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='180' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Pointer Error Matching</text><rect x='20' y='48' width='220' height='36' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>X: malloc then assign NULL</text><rect x='260' y='48' width='240' height='36' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>3: Lost Memory (leak)</text><rect x='20' y='94' width='220' height='36' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Y: free then access</text><rect x='260' y='94' width='240' height='36' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>1: Dangling Pointer</text><rect x='20' y='140' width='220' height='36' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='164' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Z: undeclared ptr, deref</text><rect x='260' y='140' width='240' height='36' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='164' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2: Uninitialized Pointer</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-row table matching X to 'lost memory', Y to 'dangling pointer', and Z to 'uninitialized pointer'.

given.aim: Correctly match each code snippet with the type of pointer error it demonstrates.

terms.meaning: Memory is allocated on the heap, but the pointer m is overwritten with NULL before freeing the memory.
terms.example: The 5 bytes allocated by malloc are now inaccessible — this is a memory leak.

terms.meaning: After freeing the memory pointed to by a, the code still accesses the freed memory through the same pointer.
terms.example: a is now a dangling pointer — it points to deallocated memory.

terms.meaning: p is declared as a pointer to char but never assigned a valid address; then *p is dereferenced.
terms.example: p holds a garbage value from the stack; writing to *p is undefined behavior.

given.plan: Analyze each code snippet: X loses the malloc'd memory (leak -> 3), Y uses freed memory (dangling -> 1), Z uses an uninitialized pointer (-> 2). Answer: X-3, Y-1, Z-2.

steps.title: Analyze X: m = malloc(5); m = NULL;
steps.apply: malloc(5) allocates 5 bytes on the heap and returns a pointer stored in m. Then m = NULL overwrites the pointer without calling free(). The heap memory is now unreachable — a classic memory leak. This matches category 3: 'lost memory'.
steps.note: Always free heap memory before reassigning or losing the pointer.

steps.title: Analyze Y: free(a); a->value = 5;
steps.apply: free(a) releases the memory pointed to by a back to the heap. The pointer a still holds the old address, making it a dangling pointer. Accessing a->value = 5 after free is undefined behavior. This matches category 1: 'using dangling pointers'.
steps.note: After free(), set the pointer to NULL to avoid accidental dangling pointer usage.

steps.title: Analyze Z: char *p; *p = 'a';
steps.apply: p is declared as a pointer to char but is never initialized to point to any valid memory. Dereferencing *p = 'a' writes to whatever garbage address p holds. This matches category 2: 'using uninitialized pointers'.
steps.note: Always initialize pointers before dereferencing: either p = &some_var or p = malloc(...).

steps.title: Select the matching option
steps.apply: Matching: X->3, Y->1, Z->2. This corresponds to option D: X-3, Y-1, Z-2.
steps.note: None of the other options have all three pairs correct simultaneously.

questions.formula_note: Pointer error taxonomy: dangling pointer (access after free), memory leak (lost pointer to allocated memory), uninitialized pointer (dereference without valid address).

questions.to_find: Which functions are likely to cause pointer-related problems?

understand.plain: Three functions each return a pointer to an integer. P1 returns the address of a local variable (which is destroyed when the function returns — dangling pointer). P2 uses an uninitialized pointer (px is never assigned a valid address before dereferencing). P3 correctly allocates heap memory using malloc, assigns to it, and returns the pointer — this is the correct way to return a pointer from a function.

keywords.explain: Local variables live on the stack and are destroyed when their function returns. Heap memory (allocated with malloc) persists until explicitly freed.
keywords.example: int x = 10 in P1 is on the stack; after g() returns, &x is invalid.

keywords.explain: A classic C bug: when a function returns, its stack frame is popped. Any pointer to a local variable becomes a dangling pointer.
keywords.example: P1: return (&x); where x is a local int — the caller receives an invalid address.

keywords.explain: Using a pointer variable that has never been assigned a valid memory address leads to undefined behavior when dereferenced.
keywords.example: P2: int *px; *px = 10; — px was never set to a valid address.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='540' height='210'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe width='150' height='130' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='95' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P1</text><text x='95' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>return &local_var</text><text x='95' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>PROBLEM:</text><text x='95' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Dangling ptr</text><text x='95' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(stack freed)</text><rect x='190' y='50' width='150' height='130' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='265' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P2</text><text x='265' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>*px=10 (uninit)</text><text x='265' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>PROBLEM:</text><text x='265' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Uninit pointer</text><text x='265' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(garbage addr)</text><rect x='360' y='50' width='150' height='130' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='435' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P3</text><text x='435' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>malloc + assign</text><text x='435' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>SAFE</text><text x='435' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Heap memory</text><text x='435' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(valid ptr)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-panel diagram: P1 has a dangling pointer problem (returns address of stack variable), P2 has an uninitialized pointer problem, P3 is safe (uses malloc for heap memory).

given.aim: Identify which of the three functions P1, P2, P3 are likely to cause pointer problems.

terms.term: P1: return (&x) where x is local int
terms.meaning: x lives on the stack; when g() returns, x's stack frame is destroyed. The returned pointer points to freed stack memory.
terms.example: Caller: int *p = g(); *p = 20; — undefined behavior as x no longer exists.
terms.connects: P1 causes a dangling pointer problem.

terms.meaning: px is a pointer declared but never initialized. It holds an indeterminate (garbage) address. Writing to *px is undefined behavior.
terms.example: px could point anywhere — might crash or corrupt memory.
terms.connects: P2 causes an uninitialized pointer problem.

terms.meaning: malloc allocates memory on the heap. The pointer px is valid and points to properly allocated memory. Returning px is safe.
terms.example: The caller receives a valid heap pointer. Memory persists until freed.
terms.connects: P3 is correct and does not cause pointer problems (though the caller must free() it).

given.plan: Check each function: P1 returns address of local variable (stack) -> dangling pointer problem. P2 dereferences uninitialized pointer -> undefined behavior. P3 uses malloc properly -> safe. Therefore only P1 and P2 cause problems.

steps.title: Analyze P1: returning address of a local variable
steps.apply: x is a local variable in function g(). It lives on the stack. When g() returns, the stack frame is popped and x no longer exists. The returned pointer &x is now a dangling pointer — it points to a location that may be overwritten by subsequent function calls. This is a well-known undefined behavior in C.
steps.note: The compiler may even warn: 'function returns address of local variable'.

steps.title: Analyze P2: dereferencing an uninitialized pointer
steps.apply: px is declared as a pointer to int, but is never assigned a valid memory address. It holds whatever garbage value was on the stack. The statement *px = 10 attempts to write to this garbage address — this is undefined behavior and likely causes a segmentation fault or memory corruption.
steps.note: Always initialize pointers before use: px = &some_var; or px = malloc(sizeof(int));.

steps.title: Analyze P3: proper heap allocation
steps.apply: malloc(sizeof(int)) allocates memory on the heap and returns a valid pointer. px is properly initialized before being dereferenced with *px = 10. The heap memory persists after g() returns. The caller receives a valid pointer. P3 is correct (though the caller should eventually call free(px)).
steps.note: P3 is the correct pattern for returning a pointer to dynamically allocated memory from a function.

steps.title: Determine the answer
steps.apply: P1 causes a dangling pointer problem. P2 causes an uninitialized pointer problem. P3 is safe. Therefore only P1 and P2 are likely to cause problems. This matches option C: Only P1 and P2.

questions.formula_note: Stack vs heap lifetime: local variables (stack) are destroyed on function return; heap memory (malloc) persists until freed. Uninitialized pointers hold garbage addresses and must never be dereferenced.
